content:
Right now Rust can ergonomically propagate errors silently (? operator), or noisily oops (.unwrap(), .expect()). However, in the kernel, we often have situations where certain conditions indicate a bug, but we don't want to oops since that would guarantee a crash. That's what the C WARN() macro is for, but it's not abstracted yet.

Since Rust has excellent error handling, we want to propagate as much as possible, but we also want to be noisy when "impossible" things happen. Right now there is no ergonomic way to do that. At most you can use if is_err() { warn(...) } or map_err(|_| warn(...)) but those aren't great...

How about adding a WARN equivalent to expect()? It could be implemented both as a trait and as a macro:

macro_rules! require {
    ($($i:tt)*) => {
        ($($i)*).map_err(|_| {
            println!("WARNING! {}:{}", file!(), line!()); // Would be bindings::warn_slowpath_fmt() or whatever
        })
    };
}

impl<T, E> Require for Result<T, E> {
    fn require(self, msg: &str) -> Self {
        if self.is_err() {
            println!("WARNING! {}", msg); // Would be bindings::warn_slowpath_fmt() or whatever
        }
        self
    }
}

trait Require {
    fn require(self, msg: &str) -> Self;
}

fn bar() -> Result<(), ()> {
    let a = foo().require("expected something")?;
    let b = require!(foo())?;

    Ok(())
}

The trait option looks more ergonomic and directly mirrors expect(), which is nice. The downside is that since it's not a macro, it can't capture file and line information (which in principle the C backend for WARN wants, though we could just let that default to something). The macro option can capture file and line information, so it's more suitable for cases without a message where the file and line information is the only thing leading you directly to the line of code it came from. Personally I would kind of want to have both (method version with a custom message and no file:line, and macro version with no message but automatic file:line)...

Any thoughts on the idea and the name?
Actually, looking into it more, it seems the file/line info stuff is done via arch-specific section tricks, so we'd probably need arch-specific code to implement it similarly in Rust...

Any thoughts on the idea and the name?

Quick note about naming: when we had similar cases (e.g. pr_* macros), and we wanted to improve a bit on some APIs/names/... before the initial merge, we were told to use the original C names if it made sense / as much as possible.

Right, but in this case it's different since these operate on Result<> types which is not something C has (and the C-side WARN etc don't operate on C int errnos either), so I think it makes sense to use a different name. We can still have plain WARN() or WARN_ON() functions or macros that do what the C ones do of course, but what I'm trying to solve here is a more specific Rust ergonomics thing ^^
I actually thought of using .warn() as the trait function before, but it feels a bit weird since it's only conditionally warning?
Re the asm, I think we need feature(asm_const) to implement the file/line stuff in Rust properly, but it should be possible with that.

Asahi Lina said:

I actually thought of using .warn() as the trait function before, but it feels a bit weird since it's only conditionally warning?

Yeah, it is, though given WARN also does it conditionally, I guess it could make sense in that way.
I guess we would want Rust postfix macros here.
Yeah, postfix macros would be ideal... but it looks like that isn't even unstable yet ^^;;

Miguel Ojeda said:

Yeah, it is, though given WARN also does it conditionally, I guess it could make sense in that way.

Yeah, it does kind of make sense. The main difference is warning on a condition vs. an Err...

warn is shorter anyway, so if people prefer it I'm fine with that!
warn_on_err() maybe?

I think that's a bit too long... since this is about encouraging people to always do proper error handling, and ideally it is used pervasively, I think we want something that is a single world ideally.
it should only be used in places where err is unexpected but can still be handled, right? In places where we know a err may happen and we handle it, we don’t want to use it?

Yeah, the idea is that it would replace all usage of expect() or unwrap() in codepaths where error handling is possible and safe. So it would be for cases where invariants have been violated (indicating a bug), but upholding those invariants is not safety critical. I have a lot of those...

Boqun Feng said:

warn_on_err() maybe?

We may need a poll again :)

By the way, that name sounds good to me (though long, yeah) if I didn't know about ẀARN_ON, but WARN_ON doesn't take a message.

Asahi Lina said:

Actually, looking into it more, it seems the file/line info stuff is done via arch-specific section tricks, so we'd probably need arch-specific code to implement it similarly in Rust...

Asahi Lina said:

Re the asm, I think we need feature(asm_const) to implement the file/line stuff in Rust properly, but it should be possible with that.

I cannot follow why we need this, cant we just use file! and line!?
or are you talking about the trait impl? because then it makes sense.

The WARN implementation on most architectures uses assembly to build up a data structure in a special section, and then reference it from the warn location. That data structure needs a pointer to the file string (which can be done with asm sym), and a literal line number which needs asm const.
You'd use file!() to create a static string with the filename, and then reference it via asm sym, and then use line!() as an argument to asm const.

I think Lina's point is most archs implement WARN()/BUG() as some sort of trapping (e.g. on ARM64 is brk instruction), and in order to pass the pointer to file!() string literal, an asm section is used per WARN()/BUG() "callsite", since that's how to pass parameters to trapping.
You could use concat! to create have the static defined inside the asm.
I'm kind of surprised concat!() works with asm!() but it looks like it does, so it looks like we can avoid the feature that way ^^

You don't even need concat!(). asm!() accepts multiple string literals separated by commas. Any of them can also be a macro invocation expanding to a string literal. asm!() will internally concat all given string literals. This was done to help with macro expansed asm!().
But line!() is not a string literal, it's a number, so you still need concat!() for stringification right?
That is possible. I didn't check.

Asahi Lina said:

But line!() is not a string literal, it's a number, so you still need concat!() for stringification right?

yeah line! return a not string value, you should conver it in a strin gin some way. Ideally here we can use a macro like 

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! warn {
    ($when: expr, $tok: expr, $($msg:tt)*) => {
        if $when {
            use $crate::kdiagnostic::KDiagnInfo;
            let msg = format!($($msg)*);
            KDiagnInfo::new(&msg, $tok, line!().to_string(), file!().to_string()).warn().emit()
        }
    };
}

this is how I implement the diagnostic in kproc_macro that sone I will make a 0.0.1 version

I guess in you case you need just 

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! warn {
    ($tok: expr, $($msg:tt)*) => {
        if $tok.is_err() {
            use $crate::kdiagnostic::KDiagnInfo;
            let msg = format!($($msg)*);
            ....
        }
    };
}

That doesn't work with asm!() since you can't have actual expressions... and stringify!() doesn't work either because it seems to stringify its argument without further macro resolution, plus discrete strings passed to asm!() become separate statements, they aren't just concatenated together. Only concat!() works... https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=5163da80dc020d6f56ace61ad57164a9
mh that it is interesting
